title: British custard tart
subtitle: Baked egg custard in a shortcrust case, finished with nutmeg
excerpt: A baked egg custard in a blind-baked shortcrust case, set just to a trembling cream and finished with a grating of nutmeg. The British ancestor of the Portuguese pastel de nata. Makes one 23 cm tart.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: baked egg custard tarts documented as a household standard, with a plain shortcrust case and a filling of eggs, cream, and sugar grated with nutmeg. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): custard tarts appear in the regional pastry entries as a British bakery and household staple. The form recorded here is the plain custard tart, without the caramelised top of the Portuguese version.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The tart is best eaten the day it is made, or the following day cold from the fridge.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 170°C in a conventional oven

glossaryTerms.definition: A custard set by baking rather than stirring over heat. Eggs and cream are mixed with sugar, poured into a case or dish, and baked at a low temperature until the proteins in the egg just set. The key is gentle heat: too hot and the eggs scramble and the custard weeps; too slow and the texture never becomes fully smooth. A properly baked custard trembles at the centre when the tin is moved but does not flow.

content.text: tart is done before it looks done. Pull it when the edges are set and the centre still trembles. The residual heat finishes it as it cools. A fully set, non-trembling custard when it comes from the oven means it has curdled slightly inside and will be watery when cut.
Make and blind bake the pastry

Rub the cold {{unsalted-butter}} into the {{plain-flour}} and {{icing-sugar}} until the mixture resembles fine crumbs. Add the {{eggs}} (pastry yolk) and 1–2 tbsp cold water and press to a smooth dough. Wrap and chill for 30 minutes. Preheat the oven to 180°C fan. Roll to 3 mm thick and line a 23 cm deep tart tin. Blind bake with paper and beans for 15 minutes, remove beans and paper, and bake for 5 more minutes. Reduce the oven to 160°C fan.
Make the custard and bake — 30–35 minutes

Warm the {{double-cream}}, {{whole-milk}}, and {{caster-sugar}} gently in a saucepan until the sugar dissolves. Remove from the heat. Whisk the whole {{eggs}} and {{eggs}} (yolks) with the {{vanilla-extract}} in a bowl, then pour the warm cream mixture over while whisking. Strain through a sieve into a jug.

Pour the custard into the warm pastry case. Grate {{nutmeg-whole}} generously over the surface. Bake at 160°C fan for 30–35 minutes, until the edges are set and the centre trembles. Cool in the tin for 1 hour before releasing. Serve at room temperature or cold.
